core_behavior.note: Models differ significantly in scope and granularity of what constitutes loggable activity, with Model B enumerating 15 distinct event categories while Models A and C use broader, less structured definitions; none define 'activity' rigorously per the requirement, and PII handling is unaddressed.

inputs.note: Model B specifies 19 structured fields including UUID event_id, previous/new value snapshots, geo_location, and delegated_by, while Models A and C list far fewer fields without formal typing; PII-laden fields like form data and previous_value are captured without PII handling guidance.

outputs.note: Model B specifies an append-only dedicated log store with a queryable internal API supporting pagination, sorting, and CSV/JSON export, while Models A and C describe generic persistent storage without API or query specifications.

assumptions.note: Model B has an empty assumptions array while Models A and C each list multiple assumptions including retention periods (90 days vs 365 days) and storage technology choices.

failure_modes.note: Model B has an empty failure_modes array while Models A and C specify retry/queue strategies and administrator alerting behaviors for logging failures.

edge_cases.note: Model B has an empty edge_cases array while Models A and C address anonymous users, high-volume activity, and simultaneous multi-user actions.

security_constraints.note: Models A and C specify encryption at rest and in transit with access controls, while Model B focuses on append-only store separation and access restriction without specifying encryption; none of the models address PII handling policy in logs.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies less than 5ms latency per log entry, Model C specifies no more than 100ms latency, and Model B specifies only a qualitative non-interruption requirement with no numeric threshold.

summary: OMI and ASM each appeared three times across the eight fields, making them the joint most frequent taxonomy categories in this report.
